# Thunder Mind ⚡ — benchmark

All numbers below are **real, measured** by `node engine/tools/bench.mjs` on this machine (Node 22).
Reproduce them with the commands in [Rerun](#rerun). No numbers are invented.

## Method

thunder-mind indexes **decisions**, not code, so the benchmark answers a different question than the
framework plugins: *how cheaply can an AI find and reuse an existing decision instead of re-deriving (and
possibly contradicting) it?*

Data-token methodology (same honesty rules as the other Thunder plugins):
- **thunder tok** = the `recall` payload actually ingested (`bytes / 4`).
- **scan-corpus baseline** = what you must read to *find* a decision **without a pre-built index**: you
  cannot know which file holds the answer, so you scan the decision corpus. This is the "read-to-search"
  cost the index removes — and it grows with the repo.
- **single-file baseline** = `bytes/4` of the one decision file that answers (a conservative per-answer
  floor: what it would cost if you already knew exactly which file to open).
- All figures **exclude** the fixed harness/sub-agent overhead (~10.6k/agent) and the SKILL.md size — and
  `recall` answers **inline (0 sub-agents)**, so that ~10.6k is avoided entirely on every query.

Test bed: a synthetic corpus generated by `gen-minddemo.mjs` (10 domains, 4 types, ~11% superseded),
plus the committed `minddemo` corpus for the worked example.

## Results — gain grows with the decision count

### Scale — build + recall stay flat as the corpus grows

| decisions | build (ms) | corpus tok | avg recall payload tok | avg recall (ms) |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 50 | 11 | 8151 | 329 | 0.09 |
| 500 | 26 | 81866 | 317 | 0.10 |
| 2000 | 81 | 328462 | 318 | 0.32 |

The recall payload is **flat (~318 tok)** and recall stays **sub-millisecond** even at 2000 decisions —
because retrieval goes through an inverted index + an idf-weighted funnel that scores only candidates and
enriches only the top 1–3, never the whole corpus.

### Recall vs read-to-search (find one decision WITHOUT an index)

| decisions | recall tok | scan-corpus baseline tok | factor | (single-file baseline tok) |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 50 | 329 | 8151 | **~25×** | 161 |
| 500 | 317 | 81866 | **~258×** | 158 |
| 2000 | 318 | 328462 | **~1033×** | 159 |

*(8 representative queries averaged per size.)* The more a project decides, the more an index is worth:
at 2000 decisions, one `recall` costs **~1000× fewer tokens** than scanning the decisions to find the one
that answers.

## Concrete example (query → what recall returns → the answer)

> Q: *"How do we isolate tenant data?"* — `node engine/thunder.mjs recall "tenant isolation" minddemo`

```yaml
query: tenant isolation
matched: 1
shown: 1
cards:
  -
    score: 1
    id: "auth/2026-06-27-tenant-isolation-rls"
    type: architecture
    status: active
    domain: auth
    title: "Tenant isolation via Postgres Row-Level Security"
    context: "Multi-tenant SaaS; tenant data must be isolated without duplicating schemas."
    decision: "Use Postgres Row-Level Security with a per-table policy keyed on tenant_id."
    rationale: "Isolation enforced at the data layer, impossible to bypass from the application."
    consequences: [Every request must set the app.tenant_id GUC at the start of the transaction.]
    alternatives:
      - {choice: schema per tenant, rejected_because: "schema-count explosion at scale"}
    supersedes: "auth/2026-05-10-tenant-isolation-app-filter"
    confidence: high
```

The single enriched card carries the decision, the reasoning, the consequence, the rejected alternative,
and the fact that it **superseded** an earlier approach — everything a second developer's AI needs to
*align* instead of inventing a different isolation scheme. The superseded decision is hidden by default.

## Tier-3 layer (answer cache + tool-output pruning)

Shared, language-agnostic layer (`engine/lib/common/`), measured here on thunder-mind:

| layer | without | with | saved |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| answer cache (repeat recall) | 307 tok | 28 tok | **91%** |
| prune (1000-line log) | 3226 tok | 165 tok | **95%** (kept 46/1000, every diagnostic line) |

The answer cache is freshness-gated by each decision's content hash + the engine hash, so a cached answer
is never returned after the underlying decision changes. Debug mode (`.thunder/mind/.config` →
`DEBUG=true`) appends a per-operation token-gain trace to `.thunder/gains.md`; zero overhead when off.

## Honest nuances

- **For a single, already-known decision the token gain is neutral or slightly negative** — the enriched
  card (~318 tok) is comparable to the one decision file (~159 tok). thunder-mind's value is **finding**
  the right decision among many and **preventing divergence**, not compressing one file you already know.
- The headline gain is the **read-to-search** factor, which only materializes once the project has
  accumulated decisions — it is small on a 5-decision repo and large on a 2000-decision one.
- The **alignment / divergence-avoided** value (below) is the real point and is not fully captured by any
  token count.

## Divergence avoided (the real point)

The decision that doesn't get *re-litigated* or *contradicted* is the main payoff and is qualitative:
- Two developers share one committed decision index; `recall` runs **before** a choice is made, so both
  AIs reuse the same decision instead of inventing conflicting ones.
- `record` refuses a near-duplicate of an existing active decision unless it explicitly `supersedes` it
  (measured: the dedup gate fires at a recall score ≥ 0.5 in the same domain).
- `conflicts` / `review` surface contradictions (`conflicts_with` an active decision), un-flipped
  supersede chains, dangling references, and code-evidence drift before they cause rework.

## One-time / amortized costs

- **Technical layer (build) = 0 model tokens** — pure CPU; 11–81 ms for 50–2000 decisions, incremental
  afterwards (only changed files re-parsed).
- **Capturing a decision** uses the scribe agent once (normalize + conflict-check). That cost is **paid
  once, committed, and shared** — the second developer reuses it for free and never re-derives it.

## Verdict

| Query type | thunder-mind benefit |
|---|---|
| "What did we decide about X?" | One `recall`, ~318 tok inline, vs scanning the corpus (up to ~1000×). |
| "Should we do X / which lib/pattern?" | Reuse the existing decision instead of inventing a divergent one. |
| "Is our decision log consistent?" | `conflicts` / `review` flag contradictions and drift. |
| Single known decision, small repo | Little/no token gain — value is alignment, not compression. |

The gain grows with the number of decisions and with team size; the cost shifts to a one-time, shared,
free-to-reuse index. thunder-mind removes "read-to-search" over decisions and, more importantly, keeps two
developers' AIs from diverging.

## Rerun

```bash
node engine/tools/bench.mjs 50 500 2000     # scale + read-to-search + Tier-3 tables
node engine/thunder.mjs --selftest          # integration test on minddemo
node --test                                 # unit tests (round-trip, recall, conflicts, shared Tier-3)
```
